3:15.rendering: That is why the promise depends on faith, so that it may be according to grace and be guaranteed to all the offspring, not only to those who are of the law but also to those who share the faith of Abraham, who is the father of us all.
3:15.note: Paul weaves Genesis 17's "father of many nations" with the single word "therefore," showing how grace turns one man's trusting obedience into a worldwide family inheritance.

2:24.rendering: God presented him as the place of atonement, through his faithfulness, in his blood, to demonstrate God's justice, because in his forbearance he had passed over the sins previously committed.
2:24.note: The Greek word hilasterion echoes the mercy seat atop the ark where blood was sprinkled on the Day of Atonement, showing God himself provides the costly covering for sins he had patiently overlooked.

2:25.rendering: He did this to demonstrate his justice at the present time, so that he himself is just and the one who declares righteous the person who has faith in Jesus.
2:25.note: Paul coins a striking new phrase, "the justifier of the one who has faith," echoing God's self-revelation as the righteous judge throughout the Old Testament.

2:26.rendering: Where then is boasting? It is shut out. By what kind of law? By a law of works? No, but by the law of faith.
2:26.note: Paul plays on "law" (nomos) to overturn it: the only rule that now counts is the one that excludes all self-boasting.

2:27.rendering: For we hold that a person is made righteous by faith apart from works of the law.
2:27.note: Paul's Greek word for "made righteous" (dikaioo) echoes the courtroom language of the Hebrew Scriptures, where God declares his people innocent not because they earned it but because of covenant loyalty.

2:28.rendering: Or is God the God of Jews only? Is he not also the God of Gentiles? Yes, of Gentiles too.
2:28.note: Paul's rhetorical question echoes Deuteronomy 6:4's declaration that Yahweh is one, now exploding that oneness outward to include Gentiles in the same family.

2:29.rendering: Since God is one, he will justify the circumcised by faith and the uncircumcised through that same faith.
2:29.note: Paul echoes the Shema (Deut 6:4), grounding his radical claim that the one God has one way to put all nations right: faith.

2:30.rendering: Do we then nullify the law through this faithfulness? By no means. On the contrary, we uphold the law.
2:30.note: Paul's strong no to nullifying Torah echoes Jesus' words in Matthew 5:17, showing the gospel establishes rather than cancels the law.

3:16.rendering: As it is written, "I have made you the father of many nations." He is our father in the sight of the God in whom he believed, the God who gives life to the dead and calls into existence the things that do not exist.
3:16.note: The Greek verb "calls" carries the ancient sense of summoning something into being, echoing God's creative word in Genesis 1.

3:17.rendering: Against all hope, Abraham believed in hope, so that he became the father of many nations, just as it had been said to him, "So shall your offspring be."
3:17.note: The Greek piles up "against hope, in hope he believed," echoing the exact promise words from Genesis 15:5 and underscoring faith that clings to God's word when evidence screams otherwise.

3:18.rendering: He did not weaken in faith when he considered his own body, which was as good as dead (he was about a hundred years old), or the deadness of Sarah's womb.
3:18.note: Paul's Greek echoes the Genesis 17–18 story of Abraham's “dead” body and Sarah's barren womb, turning their physical impossibility into the very stage for God's life-giving promise.

3:19.rendering: Yet he did not waver in unbelief at God's promise, but grew strong in faith, giving glory to God.
3:19.note: The Greek word for "grew strong" (enedynamothe) echoes the same root used when God "strengthened" Abraham's own body in the previous verse, showing his faith actively participated in the divine power at work.

3:20.rendering: and being fully convinced that the one who had promised was able also to do it.
3:20.note: The Greek plerophoreo for "fully convinced" echoes the assurance Abraham held against all evidence, a word that later describes the Spirit's bold witness to believers.

3:21.rendering: That is why his faith was counted to him as righteousness.
3:21.note: The phrase "counted to him" echoes the language of Genesis 15:6, linking Abraham's trust across centuries to every later reader who receives the same gift.

3:22.rendering: But the words "it was counted to him" were not written for Abraham alone.
3:22.note: Paul spots an intertextual echo: Genesis 15:6 was preserved in Scripture to shape every later reader who, like Abraham, trusts God's promise.

3:23.rendering: who was handed over for our trespasses and was raised for our justification.
3:23.note: Paul echoes Isaiah 53 here, where the servant is handed over for our sins, linking Jesus' resurrection directly to our declared-righteous status.

3:24.rendering: He was handed over for our trespasses, and was raised for our being declared righteous.
3:24.note: The Greek word for “handed over” echoes Isaiah 53’s suffering servant, while “raised for our justification” links back to the courtroom language of Genesis 15 that Paul has been unfolding.

4:0.rendering: Therefore, since we have been declared righteous by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ.
4:0.note: The Greek word for "peace" here echoes the Hebrew shalom, pointing to wholeness restored in covenant relationship, not just the absence of conflict.

4:1.rendering: Through him we also have obtained access by faith into this grace in which we stand, and we boast in the hope of the glory of God.
4:1.note: The Greek word for "access" (prosagoge) was used for gaining entrance to a king's court, turning the cross into our royal welcome.

4:2.rendering: And not only that, we also boast in our sufferings, knowing that suffering produces endurance,
4:2.note: The Greek word for boast here is the same one Paul uses for glorying in God, linking our trials directly to the story of a people being shaped for glory.

4:3.rendering: and endurance produces character, and character produces hope.
4:3.note: The Greek word for character (dokime) means metal proven genuine by fire, an image of tested reliability that echoes Israel's wilderness refining.

4:4.rendering: And hope does not put us to shame, because God's love has been poured out into our hearts through the Holy Spirit who was given to us.
4:4.note: The verb "poured out" echoes Joel 2:28-29 and the flood-like abundance of the Spirit at Pentecost, turning future hope into present, tangible love.

4:5.rendering: For while we were still weak, at the right time Christ died for the ungodly.
4:5.note: Paul's Greek word for "weak" (asthenes) carries the idea of moral and spiritual powerlessness, not just frailty, echoing how the ungodly are helpless without God's timely intervention.

4:6.rendering: For rarely will anyone die for a righteous person, though perhaps for a good person someone might even dare to die.
4:6.note: Paul uses a stark ancient honor-scale: the "righteous" (just, dutiful) versus the "good" (generous benefactor), highlighting how rare self-sacrifice was even in Roman culture.

4:7.rendering: But God demonstrates his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.
4:7.note: Paul's word for "demonstrates" carries the sense of a public proof or commendation, echoing how ancient rulers displayed their power, only here God proves his love through the weakness of the cross.

4:8.rendering: Therefore, since we have now been justified by his blood, how much more shall we be saved from God's wrath through him.
4:8.note: The Greek "how much more" (posso mallon) forms a classic rabbinic argument from the lesser to the greater, linking past justification to future deliverance.

4:9.rendering: For if, while we were enemies, we were reconciled to God through the death of his Son, how much more, having been reconciled, will we be saved by his life.
4:9.note: Paul's "how much more" logic draws on ancient covenant language, where reconciliation through a king's costly act guarantees even greater future blessing.

4:10.rendering: Not only that, but we also boast in God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom we have now received the reconciliation.
4:10.note: The Greek word for "reconciliation" here is the same one used for the "atonement" cover on the ark of the covenant, turning former enemies into friends who now boast in God himself.

4:11.rendering: Therefore, just as sin entered the world through one man, and death through sin, and in this way death spread to all people, because all sinned.
4:11.note: Paul echoes Genesis 2-3 here, linking Adam's single act to the universal human experience of death and sin.

4:12.rendering: For sin was in the world before the law was given, but sin is not charged where there is no law.
4:12.note: This verse echoes Genesis 3-11, showing humanity's universal guilt even without the Torah, setting the stage for grace abounding to all.

4:13.rendering: Nevertheless death reigned from Adam until Moses, even over those who had not sinned in the likeness of Adam's transgression, who is a pattern of the one to come.
4:13.note: The word "pattern" translates "typos," an ancient stamp that leaves its impress, quietly signaling how Adam's story presses forward into the shape of the coming one.

4:14.rendering: But the free gift is not like the trespass. For if many died through one man's trespass, how much more have the grace of God and the free gift by the grace of that one man Jesus Christ abounded for many.
4:14.note: Paul's tight wordplay on "trespass" and "free gift" sets up a stunning contrast: Adam's one act brought death to many, but Jesus' grace overflows far beyond it.

4:15.rendering: And the gift is not like what came through the one who sinned. For the judgment following one trespass led to condemnation, but the free gift following many trespasses leads to being declared righteous.
4:15.note: Paul's tight wordplay contrasts "judgment" (krima) from one trespass with the "free gift" (charisma) that swallows up many trespasses, setting up the flood of grace in the next verse.

4:16.rendering: For if, because of the one man's trespass, death reigned through that one man, how much more will those who receive the abundance of grace and the free gift of righteousness reign in life through the one man Jesus Christ.
4:16.note: Paul's tight wordplay on "reign" flips the story: where death once ruled through Adam, grace now enthrones us in life through the other Adam.

4:17.rendering: Therefore, just as one trespass led to condemnation for all people, so also one act of righteousness leads to justification and life for all people.
4:17.note: Paul weaves a tight parallel between Adam's single trespass and Jesus' single righteous act, echoing how the whole human story hinges on two representative men.

4:18.rendering: For just as by the one man's disobedience the many were made sinners, so also by the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous.
4:18.note: Paul weaves a tight parallel between Adam and the coming one, using "made" (katestathesan) twice to show how solidarity with a representative reshapes human identity itself.

4:19.rendering: Now the law came in alongside so that the trespass might increase, but where sin increased, grace overflowed far more.
4:19.note: Paul uses a deliberate wordplay: the law "came in alongside" (pareiselthen) like an intruder, only to multiply the trespass and set the stage for grace to super-abound.

4:20.rendering: So that, just as sin reigned in death, grace might also reign through righteousness leading to eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.
4:20.note: Paul weaves together the ancient idea of a ruler's dominion with the shocking image of grace enthroned, using the same verb for both sin and grace to show how Christ's victory mirrors and reverses Adam's reign.
